text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Gynecologic Oncology Service — New Patient Visit

**PATIENT:** Margaret Osei-Bonsu
**AGE:** 47 years
**REFERRING PROVIDER:** Gynecologic Oncology
**REASON FOR REFERRAL:** New patient consultation for adjuvant systemic chemotherapy following primary debulking surgery for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman of Ghanaian descent referred to Medical Oncology for initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy following successful primary debulking surgery for stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. She presents today approximately 6 weeks post-operatively and is recovering well.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu initially came to medical attention approximately 4 months prior to this visit with a progressive 4-month history of abdominal distension, early satiety, bilateral pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. She was found to have moderate-volume ascites, bilateral adnexal masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm with mixed solid-cystic morphology), extensive omental caking, and peritoneal implants on CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. Serum CA-125 at that time was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL and HE4 was 412 pmol/L. Diagnostic laparoscopy with omental biopsy was performed and pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin with aberrant p53 overexpression, WT-1 and PAX8 positivity, and a Ki-67 of 78%.

Germline genetic testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant: c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), the 5382insC founder mutation, which is well-characterized in multiple populations. Somatic tumor NGS via FoundationOne CDx demonstrated TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation (VAF 68%), somatic BRCA1 loss with LOH (VAF 82%), RB1 deep deletion, CCNE1 amplification (copy number 6.2), and subclonal NF1 and PIK3CA alterations. Tumor mutational burden was low (4.2 mut/Mb), microsatellite stable, but genomic LOH was high at 31% with an HRD score of 68 (positive), highly consistent with HRD-positive disease and strong predictive support for PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

She subsequently underwent primary debulking surgery approximately 4 weeks after diagnosis: exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants from the right paracolic gutter, sigmoid mesentery, and left pelvic sidewall, and diaphragmatic stripping of a right hemidiaphragm implant. No bowel resection was required. Complete gross resection was achieved (R0). Estimated blood loss was 950 mL; she required 2 units of intraoperative packed red blood cells. Her postoperative course was unremarkable and she was discharged on postoperative day 5 on VTE prophylaxis with enoxaparin.

Definitive surgical pathology confirmed bilateral high-grade serous carcinoma with serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) of the right fallopian tube fimbriae, supporting a fallopian tube origin. Lymph node involvement: 4 of 18 pelvic nodes and 1 of 9 para-aortic nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma. Ascites cytology positive. Pathologic stage pT3c pN1 (AJCC 8th edition). All surgical margins negative.

Post-operative CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed 6 weeks post-surgery showed no evidence of residual macroscopic disease. A small right obturator lymphocele (2.1 cm) was noted, not requiring intervention. No ascites. No new peritoneal implants.

Today's CA-125 (drawn this morning, 6 weeks post-surgery) returns at 187 U/mL — a dramatic decline from the pre-operative value of 3,847 U/mL, confirming a robust biochemical response to the surgical cytoreduction, though the CA-125 has not yet normalized. She is here today to discuss and initiate adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and to review the longer-term plan including PARP inhibitor maintenance.

She reports her energy is gradually improving since surgery. She has mild residual incisional discomfort but is ambulating well, eating normally, and managing her activities of daily living independently. She has no fever, no wound concerns, no shortness of breath, and no lower extremity swelling. She has resumed light activity including short walks.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, ongoing, improving from the post-surgical nadir. Appetite returned to near-baseline. No fever or chills.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. No change in bowel habits since surgery. Normal appetite.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary symptoms.
Neurologic: No focal deficits. No neuropathy symptoms at baseline.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias or myalgias.
Dermatologic: Healing well from midline laparotomy incision. No rash.
Hematologic: No unusual bleeding or bruising. Completed enoxaparin VTE prophylaxis course.
Psychosocial: Patient acknowledges significant anxiety about diagnosis, treatment, and implications for her children. She has been connected with the oncology social worker and is attending the gynecologic oncology support group. She expressed specific anxiety about alopecia and is awaiting the oncology wig program referral.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO IIIC — newly diagnosed (as above)
2. No prior malignancies
3. No significant medical comorbidities prior to this illness
4. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries prior to this admission
5. G2P2, spontaneous vaginal deliveries; menopause not yet reached at time of diagnosis (premenopausal; now surgically menopausal following BSO)

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy, peritoneal implant resections, right diaphragmatic stripping) — approximately 6 weeks prior to this visit
2. Diagnostic laparoscopy with omental biopsy — at time of diagnosis

---

FAMILY HISTORY

Strong family history of BRCA1-associated malignancies:
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer diagnosed at age 38
- Maternal grandmother: ovarian cancer diagnosed at age 55
- Mother: no known cancer (deceased, unrelated cause)
- Father: no known cancer history
- Two children (daughter age 22, son age 19): counseled regarding implications of germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant; daughter has been referred for genetic counseling
- No known history of pancreatic cancer or melanoma in the family

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a secondary school mathematics teacher. She emigrated from Accra, Ghana, to the United States at age 22. She is a lifelong nonsmoker and drinks alcohol rarely (less than 1 drink per month). She denies illicit drug use. She lives with her two children (daughter age 22 and son age 19). She is divorced. She has a robust social support network including family, church community, and colleagues. She is physically active at baseline, enjoying hiking and yoga prior to this illness. She is currently on medical leave from teaching but hopes to return to work within the coming months.

---

ALLERGIES AND ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS

- No known drug allergies prior to this illness
- Note: Carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction should be anticipated given BRCA1 status and planned multiple cycles of platinum therapy; patients with germline BRCA1 mutations who achieve extended remission and are re-challenged may develop carboplatin HSR, which should be monitored at each infusion

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily — recently completed 28-day post-operative VTE prophylaxis course (completed)
2. Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (for post-operative nausea)
3. Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for incisional discomfort (taking intermittently, transitioning to acetaminophen)
4. Multivitamin daily
5. No hormonal replacement therapy (counseled that hormone replacement is contraindicated given new diagnosis; vasomotor symptoms from surgical menopause discussed; non-hormonal options offered)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Temperature 36.8°C, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 64 kg, height 163 cm, BMI 24.1 kg/m².

General: Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a well-appearing woman in no acute distress, conversational, mildly anxious affect, fully oriented.
Head and neck: Normocephalic. Atraumatic. No supraclavicular, anterior cervical, or posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender at the midline incision site which is healing well; staples recently removed; incision clean and intact without erythema, dehiscence, or discharge. No hepatosplenomegaly palpated. Abdomen is now flat — ascites has resolved. No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses. Bowel sounds present in all quadrants.
Extremities: No lymphedema. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses intact.
Neurologic: Grossly intact; cranial nerves intact; no focal motor or sensory deficits. Sensation to light touch intact in bilateral hands and feet at baseline prior to chemotherapy initiation.
Skin: No rash. No petechiae or ecchymoses. Scalp hair intact (patient aware of anticipated alopecia from paclitaxel).

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC DATA

Today's results (approximately 6 weeks post-surgery):
- CA-125: 187 U/mL (markedly decreased from 3,847 U/mL at diagnosis; trending toward normal, not yet normalized)
- HE4: pending (baseline post-surgical value being obtained)
- CBC: WBC 6.8 k/uL, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, Grade 1, consistent with post-surgical state and recent blood transfusion), platelets 289 k/uL
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: sodium 138, potassium 4.0, chloride 102, CO2 26, BUN 12, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m², glucose 92, total bilirubin 0.6, AST 22, ALT 19, alkaline phosphatase 68, total protein 7.1, albumin 4.0
- Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L
- LFTs: within normal limits
- Urinalysis: unremarkable

Germline/molecular testing (previously resulted):
- Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (5382insC), pathogenic — confirmed
- Somatic NGS: TP53 p.R248W, somatic BRCA1 LOH, RB1 deletion, CCNE1 amplification, high gLOH (31%), HRD positive (score 68)
- TMB: 4.2 mut/Mb (low); MSS

Post-operative CT scan (6 weeks post-surgery, previously resulted):
- No evidence of residual macroscopic disease
- Small right obturator lymphocele (2.1 cm), no intervention required
- No ascites, no peritoneal implants, no new findings

---

IMAGING SUMMARY

Post-operative CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (6 weeks post-surgery): No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. Expected post-surgical changes in the pelvis. Small right obturator lymphocele, no treatment needed. No ascites. No peritoneal nodules. No lymphadenopathy. Surgical bed changes as expected.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with germline BRCA1-mutated high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1), who underwent successful primary debulking surgery with complete gross resection (R0). She is now 6 weeks post-operatively, recovering well, with a post-surgical CA-125 of 187 U/mL representing a significant decline from her diagnostic level of 3,847 U/mL. CT imaging confirms no evidence of residual macroscopic disease.

She is now seen for initiation of adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy.

**1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO IIIC, post-R0 primary debulking surgery — initiate adjuvant chemotherapy**

Standard of care adjuvant chemotherapy for advanced HGSC following R0 debulking is carboplatin/paclitaxel for 6 cycles every 3 weeks, as established by GOG-0158, GOG-0172, and ICON7 data. We will use the intravenous regimen given her excellent performance status and the lack of a clear superiority signal for IP chemotherapy in the modern surgical era following R0 resection.

Proposed regimen:
- Carboplatin AUC 5 IV (calculated dose approximately 525 mg using Calvert formula; target AUC 5 based on renal function with creatinine 0.82 and eGFR >60) every 21 days
- Paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV (calculated dose approximately 305 mg based on BSA 1.74 m2) over 3 hours every 21 days
- Total: 6 cycles
- Planned start: next week pending patient readiness and pharmacy preparation

Standard premedications:
- Dexamethasone 20 mg IV 30 minutes prior to paclitaxel
- Diphenhydramine 25 mg IV
- Famotidine 20 mg IV (or ranitidine equivalent)
- Ondansetron 8-16 mg IV

Growth factor support: Pegfilgrastim 6 mg subcutaneously to be given approximately 24 hours after each cycle completion, per institutional protocol for patients receiving carboplatin/paclitaxel combination.

Antiemetic support at home: Ondansetron 8 mg every 8 hours as needed for days 1-3 post-chemotherapy; prochlorperazine 10 mg as breakthrough; lorazepam 0.5 mg as needed for anticipatory nausea.

Monitoring: CBC with differential and chemistry panel prior to each cycle; CA-125 prior to cycles 1, 3, and 6 and at post-treatment restaging; CA-125 trajectory is an important parameter though we will be guided primarily by clinical status and imaging.

Patient counseled extensively on chemotherapy side effects:
- Alopecia: expected from paclitaxel, typically complete scalp hair loss beginning 2-3 weeks after first infusion; hair generally regrows after treatment completion. Patient is emotionally distressed by this prospect. Referral to oncology wig program confirmed and appointment scheduled.
- Peripheral neuropathy: cumulative, dose-dependent; patients should report new tingling, numbness, or changes in fine motor function at each visit; we will monitor carefully and dose-reduce paclitaxel if significant neuropathy develops
- Myelosuppression: risk of neutropenia with febrile complications; pegfilgrastim will be used prophylactically; patient instructed on fever precautions (temperature >38.0°C → call immediately or go to ED)
- Carboplatin hypersensitivity: more common with repeated platinum exposure; patient has no prior platinum exposure; she will be monitored closely during infusion; protocol for management of HSR discussed
- Fatigue, nausea/vomiting, constipation: expected and manageable with supportive medications
- Arthralgias and myalgias from paclitaxel: typically 2-5 days post-infusion; can manage with NSAIDs or low-dose dexamethasone

**2. PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy — planned following completion of chemotherapy**

Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and the very high HRD score (68), she is an outstanding candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy, provided she achieves a complete or partial response. This is supported by:
- SOLO-1 trial: olaparib (Lynparza) maintenance in germline BRCA1/2-mutated advanced ovarian cancer following platinum-based chemotherapy → 3-year PFS 60% vs. 27% with placebo (HR 0.30); significant overall survival benefit also seen in follow-up analyses
- PRIMA trial: niraparib (Zejula) maintenance in advanced ovarian cancer including BRCA-mutated patients → sustained PFS benefit

Options discussed: olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily vs. niraparib (Zejula) 200 mg once daily. Given the patient's baseline weight of 64 kg and the once-daily dosing convenience of niraparib, we have preliminarily discussed niraparib as a preferred option for maintenance, pending her response to chemotherapy and tolerability. We will revisit this discussion at the completion of chemotherapy. Duration planned: up to 3 years per SOLO-1 data.

**3. Germline BRCA1 implications — genetic counseling and family cascade testing**

Patient's germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC) has significant implications for her first-degree relatives. She has a daughter (age 22) and son (age 19) who would each have a 50% probability of carrying this variant. Patient's sister (age 44) is also at 50% risk. Referral to genetic counseling has been placed. Discussed BRCA1-associated breast cancer risk: patient herself should initiate enhanced breast surveillance (annual breast MRI in addition to mammography) as soon as she is through the acute treatment phase, given that bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy does not eliminate breast cancer risk.

**4. Post-surgical anemia — monitor**

Hgb 11.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia), consistent with post-surgical state and prior intraoperative blood transfusion. No intervention needed at this time. Will monitor with CBCs prior to each chemotherapy cycle. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not recommended in the curative-intent setting per ASCO guidelines.

**5. Surgical menopause — counsel and manage**

Patient is now surgically menopausal following bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. She is experiencing mild vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, sleep disturbance). Hormonal replacement therapy is contraindicated in the setting of ovarian carcinoma diagnosis. Non-hormonal options discussed: venlafaxine, gabapentin, clonidine for vasomotor symptoms if bothersome. Will reassess need at follow-up.

**6. Psychosocial support — ongoing**

Patient continues to express significant anxiety, particularly regarding alopecia and uncertainty about treatment outcome. She is engaged with the oncology social worker and has joined the gynecologic oncology support group, which she has found helpful. We discussed the overall favorable prognostic features of her case: successful R0 resection, germline BRCA1 status predicting excellent platinum sensitivity and PARP inhibitor response, and HRD-positive tumor biology. Encouraged continuation of support group and psychosocial follow-up.

**7. Return visits and follow-up**

- Cycle 1 of carboplatin/paclitaxel scheduled for next week at the infusion center
- Pre-chemotherapy labs (CBC, CMP, CA-125) to be drawn on day of first infusion
- Follow-up clinic visit prior to cycle 2 (approximately 3 weeks from today)
- Restaging CT and CA-125 planned after cycle 6 completion

**Plan discussed in full with patient and her daughter who accompanied her today. All questions answered. Patient verbally agrees to proceed with treatment plan.**

---

ELECTRONICALLY SIGNED
Attending Medical Oncologist, Gynecologic Medical Oncology Program
Gynecologic Oncology Service

Time spent with patient: 65 minutes (greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and coordination of care)
